failed.title: ISSUE_001: Percentage (20%) is the controlling affordable housing standard

FAIL if the draft uses only a fixed unit count (e.g., 100 or 106 units) without tying it to the 20% percentage.

failed.title: ISSUE_001: Recalculation mechanism if unit counts change
failed.match: PASS if the draft includes a mechanism or provision for recalculating the number of required affordable units if total residential unit counts change during design development or phase rescoping. FAIL if there is no such recalculation mechanism.

failed.title: ISSUE_007: Identifies missing performance security for Phases II-IV
failed.match: PASS if the draft identifies that the City Council Resolution condition #4 requires a $15 million performance bond or letter of credit for Phase I only, and that the term sheet and resolution are silent on security requirements for Phases II ($76M), III ($88M), and IV ($71M). FAIL if this gap is not identified.

failed.title: ISSUE_007: Includes performance security provisions for later phases
failed.match: PASS if the draft addresses performance security for subsequent phases — whether by requiring bonds/LCs for each phase, setting amounts/formulas, providing release mechanisms upon phase completion, or allowing the Phase I security to be replaced/supplemented. FAIL if the draft addresses Phase I security but is entirely silent on Phases II–IV.

failed.title: ISSUE_009: Identifies LEED certification timing vs. Substantial Completion
failed.match: PASS if the draft identifies the issue that LEED Gold certification (Resolution condition #6) typically occurs months after building completion due to post-construction verification, and that tying LEED certification to Substantial Completion could disrupt the phasing timeline (since the next phase's commencement deadline runs from Substantial Completion). FAIL if this timing issue is not identified.

failed.title: ISSUE_009: Separate LEED certification deadline post-completion
failed.match: PASS if the draft establishes a separate deadline for achieving LEED Gold certification after Substantial Completion (e.g., 12 months post-Substantial Completion or similar reasonable period). FAIL if there is no separate LEED certification deadline.

failed.title: ISSUE_011: Identifies force majeure 18-month cap ambiguity
failed.match: PASS if the draft identifies the ambiguity in whether the 18-month aggregate force majeure cap applies per-phase or project-wide, and notes that a project-wide cap may be insufficient for a multi-phase project spanning 8-10 years. FAIL if this ambiguity is not identified.

failed.title: ISSUE_011: Clarifies force majeure cap application (per-phase)

failed.title: ISSUE_011: Addresses cascading delay across phases
failed.match: PASS if the draft addresses the scenario where force majeure delays in earlier phases cascade and compress timelines for later phases (e.g., extending subsequent phase commencement deadlines correspondingly). FAIL if cascading delay risk is not addressed.

# Development Agreement Draft — Summary

## Deliverable
`output/development-agreement-draft.docx` — a developer-side draft of the definitive **Development Agreement** for the Lakemont Station Commons mixed-use transit-oriented development, between Granite City Development LLC ("Developer") and the City of Lakemont, Illinois ("City").

## Source documents reviewed
1. **Negotiated Term Sheet** (Jan 22, 2025) — principal business terms (the backbone of the draft).
2. **RFP Response Narrative** (Dec 16, 2024) — Developer's proposal, qualifications, financing.
3. **City Council Resolution No. R-2025-017** (Feb 18, 2025) — conditional approval and conditions.
4. **Construction Loan Term Sheet** (First Prairie National Bank) — lender requirements that constrain the Agreement.
5. **Lakeshore Side Letter** (Feb 10, 2025) — equity investor consent/governance rights.
6. **Phase II ESA Summary** (Greenfield, Nov 8, 2024) — environmental conditions and remediation.
7. **TIF District Summary Memo** (Corporation Counsel, Mar 5, 2025) — TIF parameters, increment projections, prevailing wage.

## Structure of the draft
- 20 Articles (Definitions; The Project; Land Conveyance; Public Incentives; Environmental Matters; Project Financing; Performance Security; Construction Standards; Community Benefits; Affordable Housing; Development Schedule; Force Majeure; Defaults & Remedies; Termination; Indemnification; Assignment & Change of Control; Lender Protections; Dispute Resolution; Conditions Precedent; Miscellaneous), plus Recitals, 10 Exhibits, and signature blocks.
- 5 tables (project summary, public incentives, sources & uses, insurance, phasing schedule).
- 53 inline bracketed drafting notes plus a consolidated index of all conflicts and open items at the end.
- Table of contents generated automatically.

## Developer-favorable drafting choices
- **No reversionary interest / defeasible fee** (Art. 14.3): eliminated the Resolution's reversionary interest (which the construction lender prohibits and which would kill the loan) and substituted a contractual repurchase option (only after all cure periods, Force Majeure, and lender cure/consent), a liquidated-damages alternative, and an enhanced completion guaranty — preserving fee simple absolute title.
- **Broad Force Majeure** (Art. 12): 18-month aggregate cap, broad definition, day-for-day extensions.
- **Cure periods** (Art. 13): 60-day monetary / 90-day non-monetary with diligent-pursuit extension, plus lender cure rights (Art. 17).
- **Developer termination rights** (Art. 14.2): expanded to include City title failure, infrastructure funding failure, condemnation >15%, and any imposition of a reversionary interest.
- **City environmental indemnity** (Art. 5.4): broad, survives termination/conveyance, covers cost overruns and third-party/governmental claims.
- **TIF** (Art. 4.2): Developer-favorable reimbursement priority (remediation and infrastructure first), good-faith TIF-extension covenant, land write-down and City infrastructure treated as separate incentives not charged against the TIF cap.
- **Assignment** (Art. 16): broader permitted transfers (internal transfers, estate planning, approved capital raises, lender foreclosure assignment), Key Person provision, 60-day cure before any assignment default.
- **Substantial Completion** (Art. 11.3): temporary CO qualifies (earlier release of performance security / next-phase clock).
- **LEED** (Art. 8.2): good-faith-efforts / LEED-Silver fallback.
- **Living wage / first-source hiring** (Art. 9.2–9.3): incorporated (Resolution-mandated) but framed as commercially reasonable efforts enforceable through lease/operator covenants, with no Developer default for tenant/operator non-compliance.
- **Lender Protections** (Art. 17): full suite (notice, cure, consent to amendments, subordination, foreclosure assignment, estoppels, TIF assignment) — required for the loan and protective of Developer.

## Key cross-document conflicts flagged (26 total; most critical)
1. **Reversionary interest / clawback** — Resolution Condition 3(h) requires it; construction loan strictly prohibits it (would render the loan impossible to close); Term Sheet silent; Lakeshore requires consent. [CRITICAL — resolved by substitution in Art. 14.3]
2. **36-month reversionary trigger vs. Phase I schedule** — the Resolution's 36-months-from-conveyance trigger would fire before the contractual Sept 30, 2028 Substantial Completion deadline. [CRITICAL]
3. **Living wage $17.50/hr** — Resolution mandates; Term Sheet silent. [Incorporated in Art. 9.2]
4. **First-source hiring for permanent jobs** — Resolution mandates; Term Sheet silent. [Art. 9.3]
5. **Equity demonstration timing** — Resolution (by Apr 19, 2025, with Clearmont certification) vs. Term Sheet (60 days from DA approval). [Art. 6.2]
6. **Assignment thresholds** — Term Sheet 50% / loan [50]% (bracketed) / Lakeshore 51%. [Harmonized in Art. 16]
7. **Insurance limits** — Term Sheet CGL $20M agg. vs. loan $25M agg. + Professional/Pollution Liability. [Art. 8.3 adopts higher]
8. **Force majeure cap** — Term Sheet 18-month aggregate vs. loan 180-day Phase I allowance. [Art. 12]
9. **Performance security dual claimants** — City (Phase I completion) vs. lender (collateral assignment). [Art. 7.3]
10. **TIF reachability** — TIF Memo projects $38–42M cumulative increment (with abatement) vs. $48M cap; Developer bears shortfall risk. [Art. 4.2]

Plus: RFP number (2024-CD-07 vs. 2024-CD-018); parcel acquisition history; parcel sizes; parcel orientation; contamination location on Parcel 016; Parcel 017 contaminants; county (Cook vs. Lake); affordable unit count (100 vs. 106); Phase I commencement triggers (internal Term Sheet inconsistency); prevailing wage scope; condemnation threshold; cure periods/lender cure rights; TIF assignment/City subordination; land write-down vs. TIF cap; City infrastructure funding source; abatement–TIF interaction.

## Open items flagged (28 total, A–BB)
Including: hotel brand; design review mechanics; community center specs/furnishing/library; phased conveyance; environmental cost-overrun allocation; remediation performer; vapor intrusion; off-site groundwater monitoring/Metra access; ESA reliance letters; $58.4M later-phase financing; TransitWorks equity; LEED failure consequences; CBA form; affordable housing covenant form/subordination; Substantial Completion definition; infrastructure schedule; later-phase incentives reopener; lender foreclosure assignment; lender's [50]% threshold; dispute venue/county; City Council approval of executed Agreement; lender closing conditions; execution authority; all Exhibits A–J; loan good-faith deposit/expiration; loan guarantors; Lakeshore 14% Minimum IRR.

**[1. Introduction and Purpose]{.underline}**

**[1.1 Authorization and Scope]{.underline}**

Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC (\"GEC\") was retained by Granite City Development LLC (\"Developer\") to conduct a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment (\"Phase II ESA\") of three parcels comprising approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) located at 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048 (collectively, the \"Site\"). The assessment was authorized by Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager/CEO of Granite City Development LLC, pursuant to an engagement letter dated August 12, 2024.

The Phase II ESA was conducted to evaluate recognized environmental conditions (\"RECs\") identified in the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment previously prepared by GEC (GEC Project No. GEC-2024-0612, dated July 19, 2024) (the \"Phase I ESA\") and to characterize soil and groundwater quality across all three parcels comprising the Site. The Phase I ESA identified several RECs associated with historical industrial operations on the property, including petroleum storage, metal finishing operations involving chlorinated solvents, and the placement of fill material of uncertain provenance on portions of the Site. The present Phase II ESA was designed to investigate these RECs through a comprehensive subsurface sampling program, including the advancement of soil borings, installation of temporary groundwater monitoring wells, and laboratory analysis of soil and groundwater samples for a range of contaminants of concern.

The investigation is intended to support the Developer\'s due diligence in connection with the potential acquisition of the Site from the City of Lakemont, Illinois, for development of a mixed-use transit-oriented project adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station (the \"Lakemont Station Commons\" project). The Site is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) under the City of Lakemont Zoning Ordinance. The Developer has submitted an application for rezoning of the Site to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) to accommodate the proposed mixed-use development. The City of Lakemont acquired the three parcels comprising the Site through two distinct transactions: Parcel 12-24-300-015 was acquired by the City through tax forfeiture proceedings in 2016, and Parcels 12-24-300-016 and 12-24-300-017 were acquired by the City through direct purchase in 2018. The Developer intends to acquire the consolidated Site from the City pursuant to a development agreement currently under negotiation.

**[1.2 Regulatory Framework]{.underline}**

The Phase II ESA was conducted in general conformance with ASTM Standard E1903-19, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and applicable Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (\"IEPA\") guidance documents, including the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program (\"VSRP\") Guidance Document (Publication 2024) and the IEPA Technical Guidance for Investigation and Remediation of Contaminated Sites. The subsurface investigation was designed to collect data sufficient to evaluate site conditions against the remediation objectives established under the IEPA Tiered Approach to Corrective Action Objectives (\"TACO\"), codified at 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 742.

The TACO framework establishes three tiers of remediation objectives for soil and groundwater. Tier 1 objectives are generic, risk-based standards applicable statewide. Tier 2 objectives are site-specific standards developed using modified exposure parameters or institutional and engineering controls. Tier 3 objectives are derived from a site-specific risk assessment. The analytical results from this investigation have been compared to Tier 1 remediation objectives for both residential and industrial/commercial land use scenarios, as well as to Class I groundwater quality standards established under 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 620.

The findings of this Phase II ESA will be used to determine whether enrollment in the IEPA VSRP is warranted for any or all of the three parcels and whether a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter can be obtained for the Site or individual parcels upon completion of any necessary remedial activities. The VSRP provides a voluntary, incentive-based mechanism for investigation and remediation of contaminated properties under IEPA oversight, culminating in the issuance of an NFR letter upon satisfactory completion of approved remedial actions.

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

## Summary

I drafted a comprehensive **Development Agreement** for the Lakemont Station Commons project, written from the **Developer's (Granite City Development LLC's) perspective**, synthesizing all seven source documents.

**What was produced:**
- `output/development-agreement-draft.docx` — the definitive agreement draft (validated against ECMA-376 schema)
- `output/response.md` — a summary of the work

**Structure:** 20 Articles (Definitions → Miscellaneous), Recitals, 10 Exhibits, and signature blocks, with an auto-generated table of contents, 5 data tables, and **53 inline bracketed drafting notes** plus a consolidated index cataloguing all 26 cross-document conflicts and 28 open items.

**Developer-favorable drafting highlights:**
- **Eliminated the reversionary interest** (Resolution Condition 3(h)) that the construction lender prohibits and that would kill the $210M loan — substituted a contractual repurchase option (only after all cure/Force Majeure/lender-cure periods), liquidated damages, and an enhanced completion guaranty, preserving fee simple absolute title.
- Broad Force Majeure (18-month aggregate), 60/90-day cure periods with lender cure rights, expanded Developer termination rights, a broad surviving City environmental indemnity (covering cost overruns), Developer-favorable TIF reimbursement priority with a good-faith extension covenant, broader permitted assignments with a Key Person provision, and temporary-CO-based Substantial Completion.

**Most critical conflicts flagged:** the reversionary-interest clash (Resolution vs. construction loan vs. Term Sheet vs. Lakeshore); the 36-month reversionary trigger vs. the Phase I schedule; the living-wage and first-source-hiring obligations newly imposed by the Resolution but absent from the Term Sheet; equity-demonstration timing; the three competing Pellegrini-interest thresholds (50%/[50]%/51%); insurance-limit and force-majeure-cap mismatches with the lender; the dual-claimant performance security; and the TIF reachability/abatement-interaction risk (TIF Memo projects $38–42M vs. the $48M cap).

The document passed the mandatory `validate.py` gate (ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness, schema validation, content-type registration, and relationship consistency).
